Acronus.desc: Ancient name (Lacus Acronius) for the western arm of Lake Constance, recorded by the geographer Pomponius Mela.

Aenus.desc: Rhaetian river joining the Danube at Batavis (Passau), forming a boundary within the Roman province of Raetia.

Anas.desc: Formed part of the Roman administrative boundary between the provinces of Baetica and Lusitania in Hispania.

Arar.desc: Caesar remarks that the Arar flowed into the Rhone with such incredible slowness its direction could barely be judged, during the Helvetii's migration.

Arsanias.desc: One of the two headstreams ancient geographers held to form the Euphrates in Armenia, described by Strabo and Pliny the Elder.

Astura.desc: Gave its name to the Astures tribe and to Asturica Augusta, a region Pliny the Elder describes as rich in gold mining.

Athesis.desc: Major river of Roman Cisalpine Gaul, flowing past Verona and Tridentum (Trento) to the Adriatic.

Aufidus.desc: Near its banks Hannibal annihilated a much larger Roman army at the Battle of Cannae in 216 BC.

Baetis/Certis.desc: Gave its name to the Roman province of Baetica; a navigable artery for exporting Spanish olive oil and silver.

Benacus.desc: Praised by Virgil for its storm-driven waves, and the site of a Roman victory over the Alemanni in 268 AD.

Bolbitinos.desc: One of the seven classical mouths of the Nile catalogued by ancient geographers, corresponding to the modern Rosetta branch.

Brigantinus.desc: Named for the Roman settlement of Brigantium (modern Bregenz) on its shore, in the province of Raetia.

Danuvius.desc: Northern frontier of the empire for four centuries, fortified by the limes and patrolled by the Danube fleet.

Dravus.desc: Tributary of the Danube that Roman geographers used as part of the boundary between Noricum and Pannonia.

Druentia.desc: Fast, braided river in Gaul that Hannibal's army struggled to cross during its march toward the Alps in 218 BC.

Dubis.desc: Caesar describes the Dubis almost completely encircling the Sequanian stronghold of Vesontio (Besançon) in his Gallic War commentaries.

Durius.desc: Flowed past Numantia, whose prolonged Roman siege in 134-133 BC ended Celtiberian resistance in Hispania.

Euphrates.desc: Long marked the contested eastern frontier between Rome and Parthia, near where Crassus's legions were destroyed at Carrhae in 53 BC.

Flevum.desc: Named Lacus Flevo by Roman writers; Drusus dug a canal linking it to the Rhine for naval campaigns against Germanic tribes.

Garumna.desc: Named by Caesar in his Commentarii as the river separating Aquitania from the rest of Gaul.

Great Bitter Lake.desc: Traversed by the ancient Nile-to-Red-Sea canal, restored under Ptolemy II and later reopened by Trajan as the Amnis Traianus.

Guadiana.desc: Known to the Romans as the Anas, it marked part of the boundary between the provinces of Baetica and Lusitania.

Haliakmon.desc: The longest river in Greece; its lower course marked the traditional western boundary of the ancient kingdom of Macedonia.

Halys.desc: Ancient boundary between Lydia and Media; Croesus was warned crossing it would destroy a great empire.

Hebros.desc: In myth, the severed head of Orpheus floated down this Thracian river to the sea after his death.

Hiberus.desc: Fixed as the boundary of Carthaginian expansion in Spain by the 226 BC treaty with Rome, whose violation helped trigger the Second Punic War.

Iordanes.desc: According to Christian tradition, Jesus was baptized in its waters by John the Baptist.

Jordan.desc: According to Christian tradition, Jesus was baptized in its waters by John the Baptist.

Kanobikos.desc: The Canopic branch of the Nile, its westernmost mouth, named for the temple city of Canopus near Alexandria.

Karalis.desc: Recorded by Strabo as Lake Karalitis, a major lake of ancient Pisidia in the Anatolian highlands.

Labeatis.desc: Named for the Illyrian Labeates tribe near Scodra, capital of King Gentius, defeated by Rome in the Third Illyrian War of 168 BC.

Lago di Bracciano.desc: As the ancient Lacus Sabatinus, it supplied Rome via the Aqua Traiana, the aqueduct Trajan completed in 109 AD.

Lemannus.desc: Julius Caesar describes it in the Gallic Wars as the boundary the Helvetii tried to cross in 58 BC before he blocked their migration.

Liger.desc: Rising near the region Caesar described in his Gallic Wars, the Liger (Loire) marked the traditional boundary between Celtic and Aquitanian Gaul.

Lykos.desc: Near this river Pompey decisively defeated Mithridates VI of Pontus in 66 BC, effectively ending the Third Mithridatic War.

Maas.desc: Caesar's Gallic War describes the Mosa rising in the Vosges and flowing north through the territory of the Remi toward the Rhine.

Maeander.desc: Its winding course through Caria in Anatolia was so sinuous it gave the word "meander" to the English language.

Margus.desc: Site of the Battle of the Margus in 285 AD, where Diocletian defeated Carinus to become sole emperor.

Marisos.desc: Herodotus names the Maris as a tributary reaching the Danube from the land of the Agathyrsi, in the region later called Dacia.

Matrona.desc: Caesar names it with the Seine as the boundary separating the Belgae from the rest of Gaul in his Commentarii.

Mincius.desc: Flows past Mantua, birthplace of Virgil, who celebrated its reeds and slow waters in his Eclogues and Georgics.

Minius.desc: Principal river of Roman Gallaecia in northwestern Hispania, named by Pliny the Elder among the region's major waterways.

Moenus.desc: Tributary of the Rhine described by Tacitus, its mouth guarded by the legionary fortress of Mogontiacum (Mainz).

Mortuum Mare.desc: Its western shore held Masada, the mountain fortress where Jewish rebels made their last stand against Rome in 73/74 AD.

Mosa.desc: Described by Caesar in the Gallic Wars as rising in the Vosges, its waters partly feeding into the Rhine near the Batavian island.

Mosella.desc: Celebrated in Ausonius's 4th-century poem 'Mosella' for its vineyards and villas near Augusta Treverorum (Trier).

Mulucha?/Malva?.desc: Ancient boundary river between the kingdoms of Numidia and Mauretania, marking Jugurtha's western frontier, as recorded by Sallust.

Murat.desc: Long identified with the ancient Arsanias, near which Tacitus locates the Peace of Rhandeia ending Corbulo's Armenian campaign under Nero.

Nile.desc: Marked the southern frontier of Roman Egypt near the First Cataract and the garrison town of Syene.

Nilus.desc: Reached by the reconnaissance expedition Nero sent around AD 61 to explore the river's course beyond Meroe.

Olt.desc: Known to Rome as the Alutus, it formed the eastern frontier of Dacia, guarded by the fortified line called the Limes Alutanus.

Padus/Eridanus.desc: Italy's greatest river, identified in Greek myth with the Eridanus into which Phaethon fell after losing control of the sun-chariot.

Pelso.desc: Lake in the Roman province of Pannonia, recorded by Pliny the Elder as Lacus Pelso.

Prut.desc: Recorded by Herodotus as the Pyretus, a river of Scythia noted during Darius I's campaign north of the Danube.

Pyramos.desc: Major river of Cilicia in southeastern Asia Minor, described by ancient geographers as depositing fertile silt and shifting its mouth over time.

Rhein.desc: Together with the Danube, formed the empire's northern frontier after Julius Caesar's crossings in 55 and 53 BC.

Rhenus.desc: Alpine headwaters region of the Rhine, the river Augustus fixed as Rome's frontier against unconquered Germania.

Rhine.desc: Northern frontier of the Roman Empire for four centuries, fortified by the limes and legionary bases such as Mogontiacum and Vetera.

Rhne.desc: At Geneva, Caesar had the bridge over the river cut in 58 BC to block the Helvetii's migration, opening his Gallic War.

Rhodanus.desc: Major navigable river of Roman Gaul, reaching the Mediterranean near Arelate (Arles) and serving as a key trade artery to the interior.

Sabrina.desc: Tacitus names the Sabrina as a river Roman forces operated near while campaigning against the Silures and Ordovices in Wales.

Sangarius.desc: River of Phrygia and Bithynia spanned by a massive stone bridge built under Justinian I, one of antiquity's largest bridges.

Savus.desc: Octavian besieged and captured Siscia at its confluence with the Kupa during his Illyrian campaign of 35 BC.

Scaldis.desc: Mentioned by Caesar in the Gallic Wars as flowing through the territory of the Nervii toward the Mosa.

Sequana.desc: Its source held a Gallo-Roman healing sanctuary to the goddess Sequana, where pilgrims left carved wooden votive offerings.

Sicoris.desc: Site of Caesar's Ilerda campaign in 49 BC, where he outmaneuvered Pompey's legates Afranius and Petreius without a major battle.

Strymon.desc: Xerxes' army crossed it in 480 BC, and the Athenian colony of Amphipolis stood on its banks, contested in the Peloponnesian War.

Suez Canal.desc: Roughly follows the route of the ancient canal linking the Nile to the Red Sea, restored by Trajan as the Amnis Traianus.

Tagus.desc: Celebrated in antiquity, notably by Pliny the Elder, for the gold-bearing sands carried down from its headwaters.

Tajo.desc: Celebrated in antiquity, notably by Pliny the Elder, for the gold-bearing sands carried down from its headwaters.

Tamesis.desc: Crossed by Julius Caesar's legions during his invasions of Britain in 54 BC.

Tatta.desc: Great salt lake on the border of ancient Galatia and Cappadocia, described by Strabo in his Geography.

Tejo (Tagus).desc: Celebrated in antiquity, notably by Pliny the Elder, for the gold-bearing sands carried down from its headwaters.

Thospitis Limne.desc: Great salt lake of ancient Armenia, at the heart of the Iron Age kingdom of Urartu.

Tiberiadis Mare.desc: Named after the city of Tiberias, founded by Herod Antipas around AD 20 in honor of Emperor Tiberius; central to the Gospels' account of Jesus's ministry.

Tiberis.desc: The river on which Rome was founded, its course central to the city's legends, commerce, and defense.

Ticino.desc: Tributary of the Po where Hannibal's cavalry defeated Publius Scipio in 218 BC, the first clash of the Second Punic War in Italy.

Ticinus.desc: Site of Hannibal's opening victory over Rome in 218 BC, when his cavalry routed the forces of Publius Scipio.

Tigris.desc: One of the two great rivers of Mesopotamia; Trajan briefly extended Roman rule to its banks around AD 116.

Verbanus.desc: Listed by Pliny the Elder among the great lakes of Cisalpine Gaul in his Natural History.

Waal.desc: Known to the Romans as the Vacalus, this Rhine distributary bounded the island of the Batavians, a tribe prized for auxiliary cavalry.
